name: Austin YIMBY Action
tagline: Build Austin. Housing. Transit. Infrastructure. All of it. Now.
mission:
Austin is at a once-in-a-decade inflection point where every major lever for the city's future is in play simultaneously. The city added over 200,000 residents in the last decade, making it one of the fastest-growing metros in the United States. The political decisions being made RIGHT NOW — in 2026 — will determine whether Austin becomes a city that accommodates that growth or chokes on it.

This is not a generic pro-housing organization. We exist because of a specific convergence of leverage points that makes 2026 the most consequential year in Austin's modern history:

The HOME Initiative fundamentally rewrote Austin's land use rules — but rules on paper don't build houses. We're now in the implementation phase, where individual rezoning fights, permitting bottlenecks, and enforcement decisions determine whether the reform actually produces housing. Organized pro-housing voices at every hearing is what turns policy into buildings.

Project Connect — Austin's $8.23 billion light rail system — cleared federal environmental review in January 2026, a construction contractor has been selected, and early field work has begun. But the project hangs by a legal thread right now. Attorney General Ken Paxton is challenging the entire funding mechanism at the Texas Supreme Court. Oral arguments were Feb 10. No ruling yet. If the court kills the bonds, the project collapses. This is the single largest infrastructure investment in Austin's history, and it needs active, organized defense.

Five city council seats are on the November 2026 ballot — Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, and 9. District 1 (East Austin / 78702) is an open seat for the first time in eight years. Steven Brown, a declared candidate, explicitly frames himself as anti-developer. The next council majority will determine whether Austin's pro-building trajectory continues or reverses.

445 acres of city-owned land at the Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses have been initiated for rezoning — potentially 5,000-15,000 housing units depending on density. This is the largest single development opportunity in Austin and it will go through contentious public hearings.

And the Travis County Commissioner Precinct 4 Democratic runoff on May 26 offers extremely high leverage: turnout will be 8-12%. A small organized group can meaningfully shift the outcome.

We fight on all of these fronts simultaneously because they are all connected: transit-oriented development requires BOTH the transit AND the housing density around it. Defending Project Connect and fighting for upzoning along the rail corridor are the same fight. An organization that does one without the other is leaving half the value on the table. We are pro-building in the broadest sense — housing, transit, infrastructure — because you cannot solve any one of these problems without solving the others.
theory_of_change: All of Austin's urbanist leverage points are concentrated in 2026. The TX Supreme Court will rule on Project Connect's funding mechanism. Five council seats turn over. The golf course rezoning moves through public hearings. Federal transit funding timelines create deadlines. This concentration means that organized action in 2026 produces outsized outcomes compared to any other year. We are not a permanent institution — we are a campaign-mode operation built for a critical window. If we win the court case, elect a pro-building council, and secure the golf course rezoning, Austin locks in a trajectory toward density, transit, and affordability for decades. If we lose any of them, the window closes and the city's growth becomes a crisis instead of an opportunity.

political_positioning.description: Pragmatic, broad-tent pro-building coalition. We include progressive urbanists who see density as climate and equity policy, moderate business interests who understand that housing scarcity threatens economic growth, and libertarian-leaning property rights advocates who believe landowners should be free to build. We are not affiliated with any party. We work with pro-housing Democrats, moderate Republicans, and anyone who thinks Austin should build things. This is not an ideological project — it is a practical one.

key_policy_positions: Defend Project Connect light rail from legal and political threats
Support upzoning along transit corridors and throughout the city
Streamline permitting to reduce housing construction timelines
Ensure effective implementation of the HOME Initiative
Advocate for the Jimmy Clay/Roy Kizer golf course redevelopment as housing
Elect pro-building candidates to all 5 open council seats in November 2026
Oppose organized NIMBY efforts to block infill development
Support transit-oriented development that pairs housing density with transit access
Protect and expand Project Connect's federal funding pipeline

active_campaigns.title: Defend Project Connect
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (May 8, 2026): Project Connect continues advancing on multiple fronts despite the pending TX Supreme Court ruling — now 99 DAYS since oral arguments with no ruling, an unusually long wait suggesting the justices may be divided or drafting a complex opinion.

• January 2026: Trump administration signed off on federal environmental review (Record of Decision) — a critical milestone for ~$4 billion in FTA Capital Investment Grant funding.
• February 18, 2026: Austin Transit Partnership approved a $60 million design-build contract with Austin Rail Constructors for Phase 1.
⚡ APRIL 15: ATP approved a $25M contract with Kiewit Austin Partnership for the operations and maintenance facility AND spending up to $230M to acquire up to 18 parcels for the rail corridor — the first major land acquisition push.
• Early field work continues along the corridor. Full construction expected to begin 2027, target completion 2033.

⚡ MAY 5: Council received a work session briefing on the citywide Density Bonus program, which will enable transit-oriented density along the future rail corridor. The DBC introduces height bonuses up to 750-1,200 feet in exchange for affordable housing. ⚡ COUNCIL VOTE EXPECTED MAY 28 (8 DAYS).

⚡ MAY 6: BEATF released its $766.5M bond proposal — includes $147M for transportation (sidewalks, Vision Zero, trails) that complements transit infrastructure. BEATF presents to Council May 28 (8 DAYS).

⚡ MAY 15 UPDATE: TX Supreme Court ruling still pending — now 99 DAYS since oral arguments, an unusually long wait. CRITICAL: A Travis County District Court trial is scheduled for May 28-30 to weigh the legality of Project Connect's funding structure. CM Ellis called ATP's planned $32M office lease 'politically tone-deaf'; ATP pulled the item. Waymo recall of 3,791 robotaxis (April 20 flooding incident) and new TX law requiring AV companies to obtain DMV authorization takes effect May 28 — same day as the Project Connect trial and BEATF presentation.

BUDGET CONTEXT: FY2027 budget talks ongoing. $26.4M deficit projected. General fund growing only ~3%. While Project Connect has dedicated tax revenue ($166M/year, $400M+ reserves), the broader fiscal environment creates political headwinds.

Legal status: TX Supreme Court heard oral arguments February 10 — NO ruling yet, 99 DAYS and counting. If the court sides with the city, the case returns to Travis County for trial. If with Paxton, additional appellate proceedings follow.

This campaign coordinates rapid response preparation for the court ruling, federal funding advocacy, and public education about what Project Connect means for housing, mobility, and the city's future.

**May 18 update:** ⚡ MAJOR: Austin City Council adopted the Light Rail Transit Overlay on May 16 (Ordinance No. 20260516-005), creating transit-supportive zoning incentives along the entire Phase 1 corridor and Priority Extensions. This is the land use companion policy Project Connect needs — ensuring dense, mixed-use, affordable development around all 15 stations. Combined with the Density Bonus Program heading to council vote May 28, Austin is building a comprehensive transit-oriented development framework. The TX Supreme Court ruling remains pending at 99 DAYS since oral arguments. The May 28-30 Travis County trial on funding legality is 8 DAYS AWAY.

**May 21 update:** TX Supreme Court ruling now 101 DAYS since oral arguments — still no ruling. The DBC public hearing is TODAY at Austin City Council — if approved, it completes the transit-oriented development trifecta: Light Rail Transit Overlay (adopted May 16) + DBC density bonus framework + HOME Initiative expansions. Today's council agenda also includes $4M from ATP for parkland mitigation related to Austin Light Rail, plus $200K for transit system design. The Travis County District Court trial on funding legality begins May 28 — 7 DAYS AWAY. Separately, council is considering $1.35B in convention center bonds today — the fiscal environment is complex but borrowing continues.

**May 23 update:** ⚡ TRIPLE BREAKTHROUGH DAY (May 22):

1. TX SUPREME COURT RULING: The court issued its ruling in Paxton v. Austin Transit Partnership — but it was PROCEDURAL, not on the merits. The Supreme Court ordered the Travis County District Court to rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea before the case can proceed. The court did NOT express an opinion on whether Project Connect's funding structure is legal. This means the fight returns to Travis County — the May 28-30 trial may be affected. The immediate crisis of a Supreme Court ruling against Project Connect has been averted for now.

2. DENSITY BONUS PROGRAM APPROVED: Council approved the citywide DBC with only CM Duchen dissenting. Developers can now build 15-60 feet taller in exchange for affordable housing. This completes the transit-oriented development trifecta: HOME Initiative + Light Rail Transit Overlay (May 16) + DBC (May 22). Austin now has the most comprehensive pro-housing zoning framework of any major Texas city.

3. DOG'S HEAD ANNEXATION APPROVED: Council approved a 45-year development deal for 2,600 acres along the Colorado River — projected $3.5B in property tax revenue. Controversial (environmental concerns, rushed process) but signals council's pro-development posture.

⚡ Combined, these three actions on a single day represent the strongest pro-building signal Austin has sent in years. The legal uncertainty continues but the policy framework is now in place.

**May 24 update:** TX Supreme Court’s May 22 procedural ruling sent the case back to Travis County — the court ordered the lower court to rule on Paxton’s jurisdictional plea first. Chief Justice Blacklock called ATP’s approach ‘a deliberate effort to frustrate the State’s appellate rights.’ The May 28-30 trial timeline may be affected. The ruling is procedural — Project Connect’s funding legality has not been addressed on the merits. BEATF bond presentation to Council May 28 (4 DAYS). Pct 4 runoff Election Day May 26 (2 DAYS).

**May 25 update:** ⚡ Texas Tribune published a major investigative piece (May 21) revealing Project Connect’s full scaling: 10 miles at $8.2B — from 26 stops to 15, no longer reaching the airport. Land acquisition costs nearly quadrupled; per-mile cost is 3x the 2020 estimate. The downtown tunnel is the costliest segment. This reporting gives opponents a powerful narrative but also clarifies the stakes — the scaled-back plan still delivers transformational transit with 15 stations, the Light Rail Transit Overlay, and DBC framework in place. Travis County trial May 28-30 (3 DAYS) — but the Supreme Court’s procedural ruling may delay proceedings. BEATF bond presentation also May 28 (3 DAYS). Pct 4 runoff TOMORROW (May 26).

**May 26 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff is TODAY — results tonight. Travis County trial on funding legality is May 28 (2 DAYS) — but the TX Supreme Court's procedural ruling may delay proceedings by requiring the lower court to first resolve Paxton's jurisdictional plea. BEATF bond presentation also May 28 (2 DAYS). ATP continues land acquisition and contract advancement. The transit-oriented development trifecta (HOME + Light Rail Overlay + DBC) is fully in place — the legal battle is the remaining variable.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff results: George Morales won ~55% over Ledesma-Woody ~45% — first new Pct 4 commissioner in 30+ years, with infrastructure-focused priorities. TOMORROW (May 28) is a massive council day: BEATF bond presentation (city staff recommend delay to 2028; CMs Ellis & Laine favor ~$350M smaller bond), Downtown Density Bonus hearing (DDB400/DDB850) extending the framework into downtown, I-35 Cap & Stitch Program update, $156.3M Certificates of Obligation, fare-free voting transit resolution, and housing tax credits. The Travis County trial scheduled May 28-30 may be delayed — the TX Supreme Court's May 22 procedural ruling requires the lower court to resolve Paxton's jurisdictional plea first. Also in HD-49: Montserrat Garibay won ~60% over former CM Kathie Tovo (~40%), running pro-education and anti-voucher.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: TRIAL HALTED. The Travis County trial scheduled for May 28-30 has been punted — the TX Supreme Court ruled the lower court must first resolve AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea. ATP continues land acquisition ($230M for 18 parcels) and construction planning despite legal uncertainty. ALSO TODAY: Council approved $104M advance funding for I-35 Cap & Stitch (construction pushed to 2029). BEATF presented $766.5M bond recommendation to council. Downtown DBC hearing held. $156.3M Certificates of Obligation for fire stations on the agenda. One of the most consequential council days of the year.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ BOND DECISION DELAYED: Council did NOT vote on the bond May 28 — formal decision pushed to July, with August as the deadline to call a November election. Mayor Watson came out staunchly AGAINST a 2026 bond ('not yet'), citing failed November tax rate election and financial policies. Staff recommend delay to 2028. Several CMs still want a smaller ~$350M parks-focused bond this year. The full $766.5M BEATF package (includes $200M housing) may be dead. ⚡ Downtown DBC (DDB400/DDB850) public hearing held — the new combining districts would allow buildings up to 1,200 feet downtown with affordability requirements. Texas SB 840 removed FAR limits for multifamily/mixed-use, so only height limits apply. ⚡ $156.3M Certificates of Obligation approved for fire/EMS stations, family violence shelter, and public safety facilities. ⚡ Project Connect legal status: Travis County trial remains halted — lower court must first rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea. If the court denies the plea, the AG gets an automatic appeal that pauses everything. If it grants the plea, ATP's bond validation suit is dismissed. The legal path forward could take months. ATP continues $230M land acquisition for 18 parcels and contract advancement despite uncertainty. 4 DAYS until June 2 primary in OC — the governor's race (Becerra 23%, Hilton 20% per PPIC) determines federal-state alignment on transit funding.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ COUNCIL VOTED 6-2-1 ON MAY 29 TO PROCEED WITH ~$390M BOND — overruling Mayor Watson's opposition and staff's 2028 delay recommendation. Watson and Duchen voted against; Laine abstained. The bond direction is parks, transportation, and community facilities — NOT the BEATF's $766.5M recommendation. Housing ($200M) is NOT in the current bond direction. Final vote on July 23. Meanwhile, the Project Connect trial has been officially punted — the case goes to the Third Court of Appeals if the lower court denies Paxton's jurisdictional plea. Legal resolution could take months. The transit-oriented development framework (HOME + Light Rail Overlay + DBC) remains fully in place. ATP continues $230M land acquisition and contract advancement. Also May 29: Council approved oversight guidelines for a new ~$1B gas peaker plant — adding energy/climate policy to the political landscape. The Real Deal published analysis asking 'Austin's builders can now build taller. Will they?' — monitoring DBC uptake is now the key indicator of whether the zoning framework produces actual housing.

**May 31 update:** Legal status unchanged — trial halted, jurisdictional plea pending. Either path (plea denied → interlocutory appeal; plea granted → suit dismissed) means months of additional delay. ATP continues advancing: $230M land acquisition, $60M design-build, $25M maintenance facility. The transit-oriented development trifecta (HOME + Light Rail Overlay + DBC) is fully in place — the legal battle is the remaining variable. Statewide: James Talarico holds an early polling lead over AG Paxton for U.S. Senate — Paxton's political future may affect his litigation posture on Project Connect. AISD budget proposal expected June 4 (4 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 (53 DAYS) — transit has dedicated $166M/year tax revenue separate from the bond.

**June 1 update:** ⚡ Trial remains halted — TX Supreme Court ruled May 22 that trial court must rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea first. AG argues ATP does not qualify as bond "issuer." FTA environmental clearance (January 16 ROD) secure. Construction target 2027, contingent on bond resolution. Texas Tribune (May 21): project shrunk to 10 miles at $8.2B (26 to 15 stops). Garibay won HD-49 (60%); Talarico leads early Senate polling against Paxton — dynamics could influence the legal fight narrative.

**June 3 update:** Legal status unchanged — Travis County trial halted per TX Supreme Court’s May 22 order requiring resolution of AG Paxton’s jurisdictional plea first. Either path (plea denied → interlocutory appeal; plea granted → suit dismissed) means months of delay. ATP continues advancing: $230M land acquisition for 18 parcels, $60M design-build contract, $25M maintenance facility. The transit-oriented development trifecta (HOME + Light Rail Overlay + DBC) is fully operational. Council in summer recess until July 23. Statewide: James Talarico holds early Senate polling lead over AG Paxton — political dynamics could influence litigation posture. CA primary June 2: Hilton (R) 27% / Becerra (D) 26% — governor’s race determines federal-state alignment on transit funding.

**June 4 update:** TribCast (June 2) analysis of Project Connect’s path forward. ATP advancing three major contract awards planned for 2026. Ground-breaking expected early 2027, lines open 2033. 9.8 miles, 15 stops. Trial remains halted.

**June 5 update:** Legal status unchanged — Travis County court must first rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea per TX Supreme Court's May 22 order. Either path (plea denied → interlocutory appeal; plea granted → suit dismissed) means months of delay. ATP continues advancing: $230M land acquisition for 18 parcels, $60M design-build contract with Austin Rail Constructors, $25M maintenance facility. Council in summer recess until July 23. Filing period for council races opens July 20 (45 DAYS). AISD budget vote June 18 (13 DAYS) — 558 positions affected, 11 school closures.

**June 7 update:** Legal status unchanged — trial court must still rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea per TX Supreme Court May 22 order. Convention center: TX Supreme Court denied Austin United's appeal April 2 — PAC organizing new petition for November ballot. Convention center on track for late 2028 opening. Project Connect legal uncertainty persists but ATP continues contract advancement.

**June 8 update:** Legal status unchanged — trial halted per TX Supreme Court May 22 procedural ruling. ATP continues $230M land acquisition and contract advancement. Ground-breaking expected early 2027, lines open 2033. Council on summer recess until July 23.

**June 9 update:** Legal status unchanged — trial halted per TX Supreme Court May 22 ruling. AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea must be resolved first. ATP continues $230M land acquisition. Phase 1: 9.8 miles, 15 stops. Ground-breaking expected early 2027, service 2033. Council on recess until July 23.

**June 10 update:** Legal status unchanged — Judge Shepperd must rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea. If denied: automatic interlocutory appeal pauses everything. If granted: ATP's bond validation suit dismissed. ATP says 'on schedule' for 2027 construction. FTA signed off on key environmental review January 16. Three major contracts expected in 2026: full 9.8-mile buildout, operations/maintenance facility, train cars. Council on recess until July 23.

**June 11 update:** Legal status unchanged — Judge Shepperd must rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea. Project has shrunk to ~10 miles/$8.2B with 15 stations (Texas Tribune, May 21). ATP expects to award three major contracts in 2026: full 9.8-mile buildout, O&M facility, train cars. Morales sworn in as Pct 4 commissioner TODAY — his CapMetro coordination priorities could influence county transit investment. Council on recess until July 23.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The court ruling is binary — win or lose — but the political environment around it is not. A mobilized, visible pro-transit constituency makes it harder for state officials to celebrate a loss and easier for city leadership to find alternative paths if needed. On the federal side, demonstrated local support strengthens the FTA application. Every week of organized advocacy improves the odds on both fronts.
next_actions: Monitor TX Supreme Court docket for Paxton v. Austin Transit Partnership ruling
Prepare rapid-response communications plan for both favorable and unfavorable rulings
Coordinate with Transit Forward on joint advocacy and messaging strategy
Draft op-eds and public testimony emphasizing Project Connect's economic impact
Track federal FTA Capital Investment Grant application status and timeline
Build coalition support from business community for federal funding advocacy letters
Track Travis County court proceedings on AG Paxton’s jurisdictional plea — next major deadline TBD

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Defend Project Connect often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If Defend Project Connect is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move Defend Project Connect forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: 2026 Council Elections
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (May 17, 2026): The District 1 field has grown to at least 8 candidates — the most competitive open-seat race in the city. Twenty candidates total have filed across all five open council seats (Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9).

District 1 candidates now include: Steven Brown (Save Austin Now co-chair, anti-developer); Alexandria Anderson (MLK Neighborhood Assoc president); Naguib 'Simon' Nahas (pro-housing advocate — supports smaller lot sizes and more building); Misael Daguan Ramos (ran for D1 in 2022 — has name recognition advantage); Kyra Lorena Rogers; Amber Karessa Goodwin (assistant DA at Travis County DA's Office); and at least 2 others. Nahas is the most explicitly YIMBY-aligned candidate so far. Brown's SAN connection remains a red flag. Goodwin's prosecutorial background adds a public safety dimension. Filing period: July 20 – August 17.

⚡ Pct 4 Runoff: George Morales III vs Susanna Ledesma-Woody on May 26 — 5 DAYS AWAY. ⚡ EARLY VOTING through May 22 (2 DAYS LEFT). Community Impact published detailed candidate Q&As on May 14. KUT published comprehensive Travis County voter guide (May 15). No Republican running — winner takes the seat. Low turnout expected (8-12%). LIV (League of Independent Voters of Texas) has endorsed Ledesma-Woody. KUT published comprehensive Pct 4 candidate profiles on May 18. Austin Chronicle published runoff endorsements. VOTE EARLY AND CANVASS.

BUDGET STAKES: FY2027 budget talks ongoing. $26.4M deficit. The BEATF released its $766.5M bond proposal with two options — the full package passed unanimously; a narrower $436M alternative (excluding housing) was more controversial. Late BEATF discussions prioritized East Austin (Carver Museum, Colony Park). BEATF presents to Council on May 28 (7 DAYS) — but council floated a $353M parks-only alternative at the May 20 work session, excluding housing. Staff recommends delay to 2028. Council voted 9-1 on May 8 to push HOME deeper into single-family neighborhoods. ⚡ DBC density bonus public hearing is TODAY (May 21). The next council will inherit these constraints and vote on: Project Connect implementation, HOME Initiative enforcement, the golf course rezoning, the bond package, and the expanding homelessness response.

**May 23 update:** ⚡ DEFINING VOTES ON MAY 22: Council approved both the Density Bonus Program (only Duchen opposed) and the 2,600-acre Dog's Head annexation. These votes are now the litmus test for candidates — their reactions reveal their true positions on growth and density. TX Supreme Court also issued a procedural ruling on Project Connect, adding another evaluation dimension. ⚡ Pct 4 runoff is May 26 — 3 DAYS AWAY. Early voting ended May 22. BEATF bond presentation to council is May 28 (5 DAYS). The next council will inherit the DBC framework, Dog's Head implementation, Project Connect's legal battle, and the bond decision.

**May 24 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff Election Day is May 26 — 2 DAYS AWAY. Final organizing window. BEATF bond presentation May 28 (4 DAYS). DBC approved May 22 and Dog's Head annexation approved May 22 — candidates' reactions to both votes are now the defining litmus test. TX Supreme Court procedural ruling adds another evaluation dimension.

**May 25 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff is TOMORROW (May 26) — last day to organize. BEATF bond presentation May 28 (3 DAYS). Texas Tribune's Project Connect deep-dive (May 21) adds a new evaluation dimension for council candidates — how they respond to the scaled-back plan (10 miles, $8.2B, 15 stops) reveals their transit commitment. AISD budget draft delayed to June 4 — the $181M deficit and 215 FTE educator cuts add another issue the next council will inherit. Filing period for council races opens July 20.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ RUNOFF RESULTS: George Morales won Pct 4 (~55%) — first new commissioner in 30+ years. HD-49: Montserrat Garibay won ~60% over former CM Kathie Tovo (~40%), running pro-education and anti-voucher. TOMORROW's council agenda (May 28) is a litmus test for future candidates: BEATF bond presentation (staff recommend delay to 2028; CMs Ellis & Laine favor ~$350M smaller bond), Downtown Density Bonus hearing (DDB400/DDB850), I-35 Cap & Stitch update, $156.3M Certificates of Obligation, housing tax credits, fare-free voting transit resolution, and rental fee disclosure. Candidates' reactions to these votes will define the November field.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Today's packed council agenda (BEATF bond, DBC, I-35 Cap & Stitch, $156.3M CoOs, housing tax credits, rental fee disclosure) is the litmus test for future candidates' positions. Filing period opens July 20 (53 DAYS).

**May 29 update:** ⚡ Bond decision delayed to July — the fight over whether to pursue a 2026 bond or delay to 2028 will be a defining issue for council candidates. Mayor Watson's opposition creates a clear fault line. CMs who push for a 2026 bond despite the mayor's objections signal a different governing approach than those aligned with the mayor. Filing period opens July 20 (52 DAYS). District 1 open seat remains the most consequential — candidates must position on Project Connect legal uncertainty, the bond debate, and the Downtown DBC framework.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to PROCEED with ~$390M bond development — overruling Mayor Watson and staff. Watson/Duchen opposed; Laine abstained. The bond direction is parks, transportation, community facilities — housing ($200M) is NOT included. This creates a clear fault line for November council candidates: do they support the mayor's fiscal conservatism or the majority's willingness to invest? Council also approved gas peaker plant oversight guidelines (May 29) — adding energy/climate policy to the candidate evaluation matrix. Filing period opens July 20 (51 DAYS). The Real Deal analysis (May 29) asks whether developers will actually use the new DBC — monitoring uptake is now the leading indicator of whether the zoning framework produces housing.

**May 31 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (50 DAYS). The defining litmus tests for candidates are now set: DBC density bonus (approved May 22, only Duchen opposed), bond vote (6-2-1 to proceed with ~$390M on May 29 — housing excluded, Watson/Duchen opposed), gas peaker plant oversight (approved May 29), and Project Connect legal uncertainty. The Downtown DBC (DDB400/DDB850) extends the framework to heights up to 1,200 feet — candidates must articulate positions on super-tall downtown development. AISD budget proposal expected June 4 (4 DAYS) — 215 FTE educator cuts and librarian-splitting across campuses will be a campaign issue. Statewide: Vikki Goodwin won the Democratic LG runoff (68%); James Talarico leads Paxton in early U.S. Senate polling. District 1 remains the most competitive open-seat race with 8+ candidates.

**June 1 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (49 DAYS). D1 has 7+ candidates — most competitive open-seat race. Ramos has 2022 name recognition (25.3%, 6,065 votes). Twenty candidates across D1/D3/D5/D8/D9. Litmus tests set: DBC (May 22), bond (6-2-1 May 29, housing excluded), peaker plant (May 29), Project Connect limbo. AISD budget June 4 (3 DAYS). Morales won Pct 4 (~55%), Garibay HD-49 (~60%), Goodwin LG runoff (68%) — pro-establishment Dem pattern.

**June 3 update:** Council in summer recess until July 23. Filing period opens July 20 (47 DAYS). Twenty candidates across five open seats (D1, D3, D5, D8, D9). The defining litmus tests are set: DBC density bonus (approved May 22), bond direction ($390M parks/transportation, housing excluded, final vote July 23), gas peaker plant oversight, and AISD fiscal crisis ($181M deficit, 215 educator cuts, board vote June 18). The gap between now and filing is the critical evaluation window. Pew Research credited Austin’s housing construction surge with driving down rents — this is the narrative pro-housing candidates should lead with.

**June 4 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (46 DAYS). AISD budget proposal presented today — 558 positions affected, 11 schools closing. Education funding likely dominant campaign issue across all 5 open seats.

**June 5 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (45 DAYS). Council in summer recess until July 23. AISD budget vote June 18 (13 DAYS) — 558 positions, 11 school closures, school police reductions. The budget vote will be a defining issue for all five open-seat races (D1, D3, D5, D8, D9). Bond final vote also July 23 — housing ($200M) still excluded from ~$390M direction. Austin Parks Foundation describes bond 'summer standstill.' The gap between now and filing is the critical candidate evaluation window.

**June 7 update:** AISD parents/teachers protested budget cuts on June 5 — drawing significant media coverage. Board vote on the $181M deficit plan (558 positions affected) is June 18 — 11 DAYS. The AISD crisis is becoming a central campaign issue for November council candidates. Bond: BEATF produced two options ($766.5M comprehensive vs $436M narrowed). Council reviewing financial policies. Final allocation through late July. 2/3 of residents support a tax increase. Filing period opens July 20 (43 DAYS).

**June 8 update:** AISD public comment hearing June 5 drew significant turnout — teachers, parents, and staff testified against the $181M deficit cuts. A new organization 'Reclaim AISD' is calling for delaying the budget. Board final vote June 18 (10 DAYS). Filing period opens July 20 (42 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 (45 DAYS). George Morales formally appointed Pct 4 commissioner June 11 (3 DAYS). Council on summer recess until July 23. The AISD crisis, bond direction, and Project Connect legal limbo are the defining issues candidates must address.

**June 9 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (41 DAYS). At least 8 candidates with 7 appointed treasurers for D1. George Morales formally appointed Pct 4 commissioner June 11 (2 DAYS). AISD board vote June 18 (9 DAYS) — 558 positions affected, budget must pass by June 30. Bond final vote July 23 (44 DAYS). Council on summer recess until July 23. The AISD crisis, housing-excluded bond, and Project Connect legal limbo define the candidate litmus tests.

**June 10 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (40 DAYS). At least 8 candidates with 7 appointed treasurers for D1. Known candidates include Alexandria Anderson (retired athlete) and Steven Brown (Medtronic, raised ~$6K). George Morales sworn in as Pct 4 commissioner TOMORROW (June 11). AISD full cut plan released June 3 — 215 educator salaries, protest rally June 5. Board vote June 18 (8 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 (43 DAYS). Mayor Watson opposes bond. Council on summer recess until July 23.

**June 11 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (39 DAYS). At least 8 candidates for D1; semi-annual campaign finance filings due in July will reveal fundraising trajectories. George Morales sworn in as Pct 4 commissioner TODAY. AISD board vote on $181M deficit budget June 18 (7 DAYS) — 558 positions affected. Boundary realignment workshops June 16 (South Austin) and June 22-23 (virtual). Bond final vote July 23 (42 DAYS). Council on summer recess until July 23.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Council members are the final decision-makers on every zoning case, infrastructure investment, and development policy in Austin. Electing even 2-3 additional pro-building members shifts the median vote on council and locks in the current reform trajectory. The Pct 4 runoff is a bonus: in a low-turnout election, 50-100 organized voters can determine the outcome, and county commissioners control key transportation and land use decisions. Early candidate engagement locks in pro-building commitments before candidates calibrate to the general electorate.
next_actions: Develop pro-building scorecard for all current council members based on voting records
Draft and distribute housing/transit policy questionnaire to all declared candidates
Publish District 1 deep-dive analysis: demographics, development pipeline, key issues
Research Districts 3, 5, 8, and 9 candidate fields and development contexts
Research both Pct 4 runoff candidates' positions on development and transit
Prepare endorsement recommendations for Pct 4 runoff (deadline: early May)
Organize precinct-level voter outreach for Pct 4 runoff (target: 5-10 volunteers, 4 weekends)
Coordinate with League of Women Voters on candidate forum co-sponsorship

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of 2026 Council Elections often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If 2026 Council Elections is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move 2026 Council Elections forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Golf Course Rezoning — 445 Acres
active_campaigns.summary:
The City of Austin has initiated rezoning of the Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses — 445 acres of city-owned land in southeast Austin. This is potentially 5,000-15,000 housing units depending on density — the largest single housing development opportunity in Austin's history. The site's size, public ownership, and location near existing transit routes make it uniquely suited for a mixed-use, transit-oriented development that could include thousands of housing units at multiple price points.

The rezoning will go through public hearings where organized NIMBY opposition will attempt to block or water down the proposal. We need pro-housing voices at every hearing, prepared testimony, and coalition coordination to ensure this opportunity produces the maximum amount of housing.

**June 1 update:** Rezoning initiation was 9-1 council vote. Actual rezoning will have extensive public hearings. Bond direction (final vote July 23) does NOT include housing ($200M excluded from ~$390M package). DBC density bonus (approved May 22) provides framework for future development on the site. Monitor for future hearing dates.

**June 4 update:** Council on summer recess until July 23. Development rules overhaul implementation underway. Real Deal questioned developer uptake of DBC height bonuses.

**June 5 update:** Council on summer recess until July 23. No new hearing dates announced. Bond direction ($390M) does not include housing. Development rules overhaul (minimum lot size reduction to 1,800 sq ft, missing middle zoning) in implementation phase. Monitor for hearing dates when council returns.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Public hearings are where rezoning cases are won or lost. Council members count speakers and read comments. If the only organized voices at the hearing are opponents, the rezoning gets weakened or denied. Showing up in numbers with prepared, specific testimony about the housing need and the site's potential shifts the political calculation. Coalition letters from multiple organizations amplify the signal.
next_actions: Monitor for public hearing schedule announcement from Planning Commission
Prepare template public comment supporting residential/mixed-use zoning with density
Coordinate with AURA on joint advocacy strategy and testimony logistics
Research comparable large-site rezonings in other cities for best-practice examples
Develop visual materials showing housing capacity potential at various density levels
Organize hearing attendance commitment list (target: 50+ supporters at first hearing)

active_campaigns.title: Ongoing Housing Advocacy
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (May 7, 2026): The Pew Charitable Trusts (March 18) confirmed Austin's housing construction surge — driven by HOME Initiative and pro-building policies — produced the steepest rent decline of any large US city from 2021-2026. Austin added 120,000 units (30% increase), driving median rent from $1,546 to $1,296 (now 4% BELOW the US median). This is the proof point for everything we advocate.

Missing middle and mixed-use zoning reforms advanced through City Council on March 26, creating two new zoning categories — one for multiunit housing (townhomes, cottage courts, single-stair buildings) and one for neighborhood-scale mixed-use.

⚡ DBC UPDATE (May 5): Council received the WORK SESSION BRIEFING on the citywide Density Bonus program. The DBC has five tiers adding 0-60 feet of extra height in multifamily/commercial zones in exchange for affordable housing (10% of units at each tier — ownership at 80% MFI, rental at 50% MFI). Two new 'combining districts' offer height bonuses up to 750 feet (lower tier) and 1,200 feet (higher tier) — a dramatic jump from the current 350-foot cap. Developers face stricter parking caps and design standard requirements. COUNCIL VOTE EXPECTED MAY 21 (14 DAYS).

⚡ BOND UPDATE (May 6): BEATF released FORMAL PROPOSALS — two options: a $766.53M package (passed unanimously) including $200M for affordable housing, and a $440M alternative excluding housing. The full package would cost median homeowners ~$100/year. Task force member Tina Cannon called the smaller option inadequate given '$10 billion in unmet needs.' Formal presentation to Council May 28 — 21 DAYS.

⚡ MAY 7 COUNCIL ACTIONS: Council approved $497K for Integral Care Homeless Health and Wellness Center. Council initiated an East Riverside/Oltorf neighborhood plan amendment for properties at S. Lakeshore Blvd and E. Riverside Dr — a rezoning opportunity in a key transit corridor.

Beyond the marquee campaigns, Austin's housing future is shaped by dozens of individual rezoning cases, planning commission decisions, and permitting processes every month. This is the unglamorous, week-in-week-out work that determines whether Austin's reform moment actually translates into built housing.

**May 18 update:** Council adopted the Light Rail Transit Overlay on May 16, adding transit-supportive zoning near all 15 planned light rail stations. The citywide Density Bonus Program heads to Council vote May 28 (8 DAYS). Council also backed six LIHTC affordable housing proposals and approved $46.1M for affordable housing construction and preservation. Over 600 HOME applications have been approved, adding 1,200+ housing units. Austin's multi-pronged approach — market-rate supply expansion (HOME), density bonuses (DBC), transit-oriented zoning (overlay), and income-restricted investment (LIHTC/AHFC) — continues to deliver the steepest rent decline of any large US city (16% since 2021).

**May 21 update:** ⚡ DBC PUBLIC HEARING IS TODAY. The Density Bonus Combining Districts ordinance is before Austin City Council for public hearing and first reading — replacing DB90 and VMU with five new tiers (DBCBase through DBC60). If approved, this completes a comprehensive zoning reform trifecta alongside the Light Rail Transit Overlay (May 16) and HOME Initiative expansions. ⚡ BOND ALERT: Council members floated a smaller $353M parks-focused bond at the May 20 work session, EXCLUDING the $200M housing allocation from the BEATF's $766.5M package. Housing advocates need to push for the full BEATF package including housing. BEATF presents to Council May 28 (7 DAYS).

**May 27 update:** ⚡ TOMORROW (May 28) is the pivotal council day for housing: BEATF bond presentation (city staff recommend delay to 2028; CMs Ellis & Laine favor ~$350M smaller bond — housing advocates must push for the full package with $200M housing allocation), Downtown Density Bonus hearing (DDB400/DDB850) extending the DBC framework into downtown, housing tax credits, and rental fee disclosure. Pct 4 runoff results: George Morales won ~55%, bringing infrastructure-focused priorities to the commissioners court.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Council approved $104M advance funding for I-35 Cap & Stitch. BEATF presented $766.5M bond to council (includes $200M housing). Downtown DBC hearing held. I-35 construction pushed to 2029. New AV law SB 2807 takes effect today.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ Bond decision delayed to July — the $766.5M BEATF package ($200M housing) may be dead as mayor opposes 2026 bond entirely. The smaller ~$350M parks-focused alternative EXCLUDES housing. Housing advocates face a critical advocacy window before July vote. ⚡ Downtown DBC (DDB400/DDB850) hearing held May 28 — would enable super-tall development downtown (up to 1,200 ft). Combined with the citywide DBC (approved May 22), Light Rail Transit Overlay (May 16), and HOME Initiative, Austin's pro-housing zoning framework is now the most comprehensive of any major Texas city. ⚡ Travis County approved $17.65M in childcare contracts — part of the $75M annual Raising Travis County fund, far exceeding earlier projections. George Morales (new Pct 4 commissioner) brings infrastructure-focused priorities.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ BOND MOVES FORWARD: Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to proceed with ~$390M bond based on staff scenario (parks, transportation, community facilities). Watson/Duchen opposed; Laine abstained. Housing ($200M) is NOT in the current direction — a major loss for housing advocates. The advocacy window before the July 23 final vote is now the critical moment to push for adding housing back. ⚡ The Real Deal published analysis (May 29): 'Austin's builders can now build taller. Will they?' — examining whether the new DBC framework will actually generate development. Early DBC applications are the leading indicator. ⚡ Council also approved oversight guidelines for a ~$1B gas peaker plant — environmental groups oppose; Austin Energy calls it 'an unfortunate necessity' for grid reliability.

**May 31 update:** Developer skepticism is the key risk — Pearlstone Partners CEO Bill Knauss and Urbanspace CEO Kevin Burns told The Real Deal they 'don't see many differences from this program and programs in the past.' CM Zohaib Quadri added an amendment to explore expanding the DBC south of Lady Bird Lake. Early DBC applications are the leading indicator of whether policy translates to housing. Meanwhile, the bond's exclusion of housing ($200M) from the ~$390M direction creates an advocacy window before the July 23 final vote. AISD budget proposal June 4 (4 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 (53 DAYS).

**June 1 update:** ⚡ DBC IN IMPLEMENTATION: Developers can seek 15-60 feet additional height for 10% affordable units. Downtown DBC (DDB400/DDB850) also approved. Dog's Head (2,600 acres, May 22) creating new neighborhoods. Bond final vote July 23 (52 DAYS) — housing excluded. HOME continues: 600+ applications, 1,200+ units. Community survey showed housing as top priority. Advocates must mobilize before July 23 to get $200M housing back in the bond.

**June 3 update:** Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to proceed with ~$390M bond — parks ($250M), transportation, and community facilities. Housing ($200M) NOT included. Mayor Watson opposed. Final vote July 23. Council in summer recess. The bond conversation resumes at the July 23 meeting.

**June 4 update:** Austin Parks Foundation describes bond 'summer standstill.' Final vote July 23. Housing ($200M) still excluded from ~$390M package.

**June 5 update:** Bond 'summer standstill' continues — final vote July 23 (48 DAYS). Housing ($200M) remains excluded from ~$390M package. DBC implementation underway — monitor early developer applications as the leading indicator of framework success. Two-thirds of Austin residents willing to support a tax increase for the bond; housing was a top community priority — advocacy window open until July 23 to push for housing inclusion. Council on summer recess.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Zoning reform is necessary but not sufficient. Without ongoing monitoring, bureaucratic inertia and case-by-case NIMBY opposition can effectively nullify reforms. Consistent presence at planning commission meetings and individual case hearings creates a reputation for organized pro-housing advocacy that shifts the default expectation. Staff and commissioners who know that pro-housing groups are watching and will show up are less likely to quietly undermine implementation.
next_actions: Establish weekly monitoring of Planning Commission agendas for relevant cases
Build volunteer rotation for planning commission hearing attendance
Track HOME Initiative implementation metrics: permits issued, units entitled, processing times
Document and publicize cases where HOME implementation is being undermined or delayed
Create quick-response system for mobilizing testimony on individual rezoning cases
Amplify Pew Charitable Trusts report (March 18, 2026) showing Austin's 16% rent decline as proof YIMBY policy works
Engage in missing middle/mixed-use zoning reform public comment process — support 4-6 unit allowances on 5,750+ sq ft lots
Track and publicize HOME Initiative implementation metrics: units permitted, rents, construction timelines

aligned_organizations.name: AURA
aligned_organizations.relationship: Strong ally — coordinate on most housing and land use issues. Deepest policy expertise in Austin's urbanist ecosystem.

aligned_organizations.name: Transit Forward
aligned_organizations.relationship: Primary ally on Project Connect defense and transit-oriented development. Co-coordinate rapid response for TX Supreme Court ruling.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Habitat for Humanity
aligned_organizations.relationship: Aligned on housing supply and affordability goals. Credible voice on housing need from direct service perspective.

aligned_organizations.name: Downtown Austin Alliance
aligned_organizations.relationship: Ally on urban density, downtown development, and transit. Business community perspective strengthens coalition breadth.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Chamber of Commerce
aligned_organizations.relationship: Issue-specific ally on housing supply and workforce housing. Engaged on economic competitiveness arguments for density.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Monitor
aligned_organizations.relationship: Independent local news source covering city government. Key information source for tracking council actions and development cases.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Bulldog
aligned_organizations.relationship: Investigative local journalism. Information source for government accountability and policy implementation tracking.

aligned_organizations.name: League of Women Voters
aligned_organizations.relationship: Potential co-sponsor for candidate forums in 2026 council races. Nonpartisan credibility adds legitimacy to voter education efforts.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Their family-sized housing campaign directly connects to our housing supply advocacy. More housing at every size enables family formation.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Safe & Sound
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Housing supply is upstream of homelessness — every unit of housing built reduces the pressure that pushes people out. Their public safety and homelessness accountability work addresses the downstream consequences of the housing crisis we fight upstream.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, local press coverage, op-eds on Project Connect and housing. Austin Monitor and Austin Bulldog articles as shared context.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Attend a monthly briefing on campaign updates (court ruling status, council race developments, rezoning timeline). Read our issue guides and scorecards.
engagement_pipeline.action: Show up at a public hearing, submit public comment, contact your council member, volunteer for Pct 4 runoff canvassing, attend candidate forums.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the steering committee, lead a campaign working group, represent us at planning commission, coordinate rapid response for court ruling.

immediate.action: Advocate to ADD HOUSING BACK into the ~$390M bond package before the July 23 final vote — submit SpeakUpAustin comments and contact council members. Housing ($200M) was excluded from the May 29 direction

Monitor early DBC density bonus applications — track whether developers are using the new 15/30/45/60-foot height bonuses. Developer skepticism (Knauss, Burns) makes this the leading indicator of framework success

Evaluate District 1 council candidates' responses to the DBC vote (May 22), bond vote (May 29), and gas peaker plant decision — these are the defining litmus tests. Filing period opens July 20 (50 DAYS)
Maintain TX Supreme Court / Third Court of Appeals ruling rapid-response readiness — legal path could take months but ruling could come any day. Talarico-Paxton Senate dynamic adds new variable

medium_term.action: Evaluate District 1 council candidates on housing/transit positions — field at 8+ candidates, filing opens July 20
Prepare testimony toolkit for golf course rezoning hearings
Publish pro-building council scorecard for outgoing members and announced candidates
Establish planning commission monitoring rotation with volunteer commitments

strategic.action: Build multi-org coalition endorsement slate for November council elections across Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9
strategic.notes: Combined endorsement slate with AURA, Transit Forward, and Downtown Austin Alliance would have significant influence. Coordinate questionnaire and evaluation process.

strategic.action: Develop post-court-ruling strategy for both outcomes — ruling imminent at 87 days
strategic.timeframe: Ready before ruling (could be any day)
strategic.notes: Favorable ruling: accelerate federal funding advocacy and TOD planning. Unfavorable: mobilize for legislative fix, explore alternative funding, maintain coalition morale.

strategic.action: Connect golf course rezoning advocacy to transit-oriented development planning and DBC density bonus framework
strategic.notes: The golf course site's housing potential is maximized with transit access. The DBC public hearing is TODAY (May 21). Advocacy for both reinforces each. ⚡ CRITICAL: The $353M parks-only bond counter-proposal (May 20) excludes housing — advocate for the full $766.5M BEATF package.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Rapid growth can feel imposed on existing neighborhoods, and residents worry that rushed upzoning or megaproject spending will raise taxes, strain infrastructure, and displace long-time communities if affordability safeguards and implementation quality lag behind policy promises.

cross_partisan_appeals.audience: Fiscal conservatives and homeowners concerned about taxes
cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Build enough homes and transit capacity now to avoid higher long-run tax burdens from congestion, emergency fixes, and sprawl-driven infrastructure duplication.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Austin added substantial housing supply and saw rents cool relative to peak levels; coordinated land-use and transportation planning lowers per-capita infrastructure costs over time.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "You do not have to love every development to see the math: if we block homes near jobs and transit, taxpayers pay more later for roads, utilities, and commute-time losses."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Housing abundance is a cost-of-living and commute-time issue, not just a developer issue.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: When housing supply expands, rent pressure eases and workers can live closer to jobs, reducing turnover for local employers and household transportation costs.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "This is about whether teachers, nurses, and restaurant workers can afford to stay in Austin without two-hour commutes."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: More homes near transit protects regional open space and reduces vehicle miles traveled.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Transit-oriented growth concentrates demand in existing urbanized areas, limiting fringe sprawl and associated emissions.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If we care about clean air and less sprawl, we should put more homes where buses and rail already go."

common_ground: Austin should remain livable for current residents and newcomers.
Public dollars should be used efficiently and transparently.
Infrastructure should keep pace with growth rather than fail after the fact.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Do not dismiss all neighborhood concerns as bad faith NIMBYism.
Do not promise that any single zoning reform will instantly solve affordability.
Do not frame opponents as anti-city or anti-family.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: Exact unit counts and affordability mix for major rezonings before final site plans.
Court and legislative outcomes that affect transit timelines and funding.
Short-term construction disruption impacts by corridor.
honest_limits: We cannot guarantee immediate rent drops in every neighborhood.
We cannot prevent all displacement risk without complementary anti-displacement policies.
We cannot deliver transit outcomes if state or federal funding collapses.
